_comment: S1 asymmetric Civil War economy sim parameter set. Machine-readable companion to HISTORICAL-DATA-ECONOMY.md. All values are grounded in the verified 1860-65 dossiers; designer-chosen scalings are flagged 'inferred' and MUST be playtested. The sim should produce the historically correct asymmetry: Union = high convertible capacity (industry/finance/rail/sea/manpower); Confederacy = high illiquid wealth + a fragile cotton-export lever that collapses into blockade strangulation, runaway inflation, and manpower shortage.
provenance: Consolidated from 8 subsystem dossiers (union-finance, csa-finance, industry, railroads, agriculture, cotton-blockade-foreign, manpower, macro-snapshot). Verified facts from McPherson, Ball, Surdam, Black, Hurt, Owsley, Gallagher, Bensel, 1860 US Census, EH.net, American Battlefield Trust. See the .md for per-fact tags + sources.

units._comment: Normalized game units. Index values use US=100 as the convertible-capacity baseline unless noted. Money is in normalized 'war-funds' points unless a real-dollar field is named. Turns: design assumes ~12-16 turns = the 4-year war (1 turn ~ 1 quarter/season). Tune turnCount in the build; per-turn rates below are documented for a 16-turn war and should be rescaled if turnCount changes.
units.money: normalized war-funds points (1000 pts ~ $1 billion 1860-dollars of mobilizable finance)
units.turnModel: 16 turns = 4-year war (~1 turn per season); rescale per-turn rates if turnCount differs

US._comment: Union: deep, convertible, replenishable. The 'long-war' side. Starts strong on every convertible axis and can sustain attrition.
US.name: Union

CS._comment: Confederacy: high wealth/low convertibility. Rich per free capita but ~44% of wealth is illiquid slave capital it cannot arm, mortgage abroad, or convert to materiel. One powerful cotton-export lever that the blockade strangles. Forced onto the printing press -> hyperinflation. Cannot replace casualties late-war.
CS.name: Confederacy
CS._slaveCapitalNote: inferred liquidity flag: high stock, ~0.05 liquidity. Self-liquidates on the emancipationProclamation timeline event.

finance._comment: Core asymmetry engine. US balances bonds/taxes/printing and keeps inflation low; CS is forced onto printing and spirals. revenueMix is the historical war-long average; treat as the AI default and the player's starting slider position.

taxes._comment: Per-turn revenue from taxation = legislatedTax * collectionEfficiency. Pulling harder costs political capital/morale.

US._provenance: verified: functioning Bureau of Internal Revenue; tariffs collected in gold fund bond interest

CS._provenance: verified: CS taxes ~8% of revenue, tax-in-kind ~$62M, ~50% below market, widely evaded; tariff gold near-zero (blockade)

bonds._comment: absorptionPerTurn = how much new debt the public will buy each turn. US has a mass-market machine; CS market is thin and shrinks as inflation rises.

US._provenance: verified: 5-20s 6% gold, 7-30s 7.30%; ~2,500 Cooke agents; National Banking Acts force banks to hold bonds (note issue up to 90% of bond value)

CS._provenance: verified: 1861 Produce Loan ~$50-100M cotton-pledged; yield rises + uptake falls as inflation climbs

foreignLoan._comment: CS-only special lever (Erlanger). One-time large cash injection at punitive cost, tied to cotton deliverability through the blockade; value decays with battlefield/blockade setbacks.

CS._provenance: verified (Gentry 1970; Britannica; S1c numbersAudit CORRECTED): 77 was Erlanger's BUY price, 90 the public offer (the 13pt spread + 5% commission = Erlanger's margin); after the post-Vicksburg/Gettysburg slide and Confederate price-propping repurchases, net realized was ~£1.4M of £3M (~45% of face, NOT the 72% first cited); worthless at surrender

printing._comment: The morality-tale lever. Cheap immediate funds, but feeds inflation. US is anchored (legal tender + tax-receivable + bond-convertible); CS is unanchored -> compounding spiral.

US._provenance: verified: ~$447-450M greenbacks; legal-tender status anchors value; ~80% total inflation

CS._provenance: verified: ~$1.7B notes, money supply x11.5 but prices x90 (velocity/confidence collapse); ~10%/month

repudiation._comment: CS-only desperation lever (Feb 1864 Currency Reform). One-time: cut money stock ~1/3 for short-term price relief, permanent confidence/morale shock, then inflation resumes.

CS._provenance: verified: notes >$5 to 4% bonds or 2-for-3 exchange; brief dip then resumes

inflation._comment: Track as a cumulative price-level multiplier vs 1861=1.0. US is bounded/dampened; CS is super-linear/compounding. Crossing csConfidenceThresholds triggers morale + supply penalties (couples to agriculture + manpower).

US._provenance: verified: ~80% cumulative; greenback gold-value low ~39c July 1864 then recovers

CS.feedback: super-linear (each high-printing turn raises next turn's multiplier; flight from currency)

confidenceThresholds._comment: paper-per-gold value; crossing each applies escalating morale/supply penalties + can trigger bread riots (see agriculture)

CS._provenance: verified: index 100->9000+; -40% real output; Sam Upham counterfeits ~1-2.5% of money supply

production._comment: Industry/rail/agriculture output. US capacity is effectively unconstrained for war needs; CS is gated by hard ceilings (iron furnaces ~20k tons/yr), single-point-of-failure nodes (Tredegar/Augusta), and import dependence for small arms.

smallArmsPerTurn.unit: thousands of shoulder arms per turn (16-turn war)
smallArmsPerTurn._provenance: verified totals: US ~1.67M M1861s (Springfield 793,434 + contractors ~882,561); CS ~107,000 domestic over 4 yrs

artilleryPerTurn.US: high (distributed: West Point Foundry et al.)
artilleryPerTurn._provenance: verified: Tredegar ~1,100 = half of CS artillery over 4 yrs

gunpowder._note: powder was NOT the binding CS constraint; metal + arms + transport were

ironCeilingTonsPerYear.US: unconstrained for war needs (~93% of US pig iron)
ironCeilingTonsPerYear._comment: HARD ceiling that throttles ALL CS metal production (rail + ordnance + armor compete for the same iron)

CS._comment: must import machinery, train labor, divert iron

newFactoryBuild._provenance: inferred from scholarship (North converts civilian plant fast; South builds from scratch under Gorgas)

importDependenceSmallArms.CS: high: majority imported (~350k-600k European arms vs ~107k domestic); gate CS arms supply on blockade-runner success, NOT domestic factories

singlePointOfFailure._comment: CS-only catastrophic node vulnerability -> strong event/capture hooks
singlePointOfFailure._provenance: verified concentration; capturing Richmond/Augusta should cripple CS ordnance

rail._comment: US network self-sustaining + can re-gauge/repair/operate captured Southern line (USMRR). CS network decays irreversibly (no replacement iron) and fragments (multi-gauge, ~100 companies).

railIronTonsPerYear._provenance: verified: CS prewar 26k (already < 49.5k need) -> ~0 under blockade + ordnance diversion

deteriorationPerTurnPct._comment: cumulative throughput loss when maintenance uncovered; derived from speed collapse 25->10 mph + 25% locos down by 1863

throughputSpeedMultiplier._provenance: verified speeds 25->10 mph by 1863; multiplier inferred

gaugeBreakPenalty.CS: high (5-ft broad gauge + standard-gauge pockets; +1 transfer per long-haul move)
gaugeBreakPenalty._provenance: verified (Chattanooga case: US ~0-1 transfer vs CS ~8)

strategicRedeployBenchmark._designRule: model US strategic rail moves ~2x faster and ~2x larger with fewer losses

usmrrLever._provenance: verified: USMRR 2,000+ mi, 419 locos, 6,330 cars, 10,000 workers; Haupt prefab trusses (Potomac Creek bridge 9 days)

agriculture._comment: US is a surplus breadbasket buffered by mechanization. CS produces enough in aggregate but cannot distribute it (broken rail) and suppresses marketed supply via impressment/tithe -> 'hunger amid plenty' + bread riots.

distributionEfficiency._comment: fraction of produced food reaching armies/cities; CS declines with rail decay

laborResilienceToMobilization.unit: fraction of output retained per 10% of male labor drafted
laborResilienceToMobilization._provenance: verified mechanization (125k+ reapers, 230k sold in war); retention % inferred

rationTargetLbPerDay._provenance: verified: 1861 US regulation ration; CS cut + cornmeal substitution spring 1862

rationDeliveryVariancePct._comment: CS commissary oscillates feast/famine due to transport failure

CS._comment: CS player choice; >50% cotton triggers food-deficit penalty

foodPriceInflationMultiplier._provenance: verified: Richmond flour ~$40->$275/barrel; couples to finance.inflation

CS.fires: urban food price multiplier > 4x AND ration variance high (spring window)

grainDiplomacyLever.US: high grain surplus -> +British neutrality / -CS recognition odds ('King Wheat'; Britain ~25% bread-grain from US)
grainDiplomacyLever.CS: cotton-embargo lever weak vs British 1860 cotton glut (~1M+ bale stockpile)

cottonBlockade._comment: CS asymmetric special subsystem. Cotton is a huge but fragile turn-0 revenue weapon strangled by a tightening blockade and the self-defeating 1861 embargo. Models King Cotton diplomacy as a TRAP lever and the blockade's decisive aggregate (not per-voyage) effect.

cottonExportRevenuePotentialUSD_perYear._provenance: verified: cotton ~60% of US exports by value 1860

CS.actualEffect: -90%+ near-term CS export revenue, 0 recognition gained
CS._designNote: TRAP lever: looks coercive, drains treasury, no diplomacy payoff (Britain held ~1M+ bale surplus)

blockade._aggregateNote: verified: <2M bales total vs ~17M peacetime (~1/9). The decisive metric is AGGREGATE collapse despite high per-voyage success.

runnerEconomics._comment: Drives huge per-voyage profit -> incentive distortion toward luxury cargo over munitions (late-war CS cargo quotas correct this).
runnerEconomics._provenance: verified: arbitrage 3c vs 45c-$1; Banshee ~700% on 8 trips

substitutionRamp._effect: per-turn decay of King-Cotton diplomatic leverage

foreignRecognition._comment: Binary diplomacy gate. CS recognition pressure rises with famine severity but is CAPPED by substitution + slavery politics, and becomes UNREACHABLE after Antietam + Emancipation (Sep 1862).
foreignRecognition._provenance: verified: Antietam + Emancipation foreclosed recognition; Trent defused

manpower._comment: The lever that ends the war. US absorbs and replaces casualties from a deep pool (+ unlocks USCT, a pool the CS cannot tap); CS replacement ratio falls below 1.0 toward 1865. Union draft is a COERCIVE STIMULUS to volunteering/bounties, not a direct manpower source.

startingManpoolThousands._provenance: verified (1860 Census); CS cannot arm enslaved without collapsing its own war aim

baseVolunteerPerTurn.CS: high in 1861, decaying sharply from 1862 as 12-month terms expire
baseVolunteerPerTurn._provenance: verified: CS curve falls first, forcing conscription Apr 1862 (a year before the Union)

directShareOfArmy._provenance: verified: US draft only ~6%; 9,881 personally held 1863. CS draft structurally load-bearing.

conscription.unionDraftModel: multiplier on volunteer/bounty inflow, NOT a direct add

ageBand._designNote: CS widening band is a visible crisis 'tell' yielding diminishing, lower-quality manpower

US.effect: drafted man exits, NO soldier gained; cash drain + political backlash

substitution._provenance: verified: ~26,000 US substitutes 1863; CS abolished Dec 28 1863

CS.effect: weak: paid in depreciating CSA currency, near-worthless late war

bounty._provenance: verified; asymmetry driven by currency strength

US._note: a NEW pool drawn from the South's labor base -> double-swing

usctUnlock._provenance: verified: ~179k-186k USCT; CS GO No. 14 yielded only dozens

CS.effect: morale/legitimacy penalty ('rich man's war, poor man's fight')

desertionPerTurn.CS: rising over time (~104,000+ recorded); accelerates 1864-65 as Union armies penetrate South

replacementRatio._comment: replacements per casualty; the decisive asymmetry that ends the war

timeline.event: 1860 Census baseline (4:1 free pop, 90/10 manufacturing, 22k vs 9k rail, ~$3B slave capital)
timeline.effect: sets all starting parameters

timeline.event: Lincoln proclaims blockade (Anaconda Plan); USMRR-precursor mobilization
timeline.effect: begins naval strangulation of CS trade

timeline.event: CS cotton self-embargo (King Cotton)
timeline.effect: TRAP: forfeits hard currency in its one easy-export year; no recognition

timeline.event: Revenue Act of 1861 (first US income tax)
timeline.effect: direct-tax precedent; slow revenue forces loans

timeline.event: Trent Affair defused; Northern specie suspension
timeline.effect: recognition crisis passes; clears path for greenbacks

timeline.event: First Legal Tender Act ($150M greenbacks)
timeline.effect: fiat legal-tender currency, anchored

timeline.event: CS first national conscription (18-35)
timeline.effect: stops bleed of expiring enlistments; begins class resentment

timeline.event: USMRR + Haupt Construction Corps established; Potomac Creek bridge in 9 days
timeline.effect: rail repair becomes a renewable Union asset

timeline.event: Blockade cuts British rail-iron imports; CS iron diverted to ordnance
timeline.effect: irreversible rail decay begins

timeline.event: Revenue Act of 1862 (progressive income tax + Bureau of Internal Revenue)
timeline.effect: durable federal tax machinery anchors greenback

timeline.event: Twenty-Negro Law
timeline.effect: morale/legitimacy debuff

timeline.event: Antietam + preliminary Emancipation Proclamation
timeline.effect: British recognition foreclosed; detonates slave-capital trajectory

timeline.event: Emancipation Proclamation takes effect; USCT organized
timeline.effect: unlocks ~180k-man pool from the South's labor base

timeline.event: National Banking Act of 1863
timeline.effect: captive bond demand + uniform currency

timeline.event: Union Enrollment Act ($300 commutation + substitution)
timeline.effect: draft as coercive stimulus to volunteering

timeline.event: Erlanger cotton loan floated (£3M; net ~45% of face)
timeline.effect: one-time foreign cash at punitive cost

timeline.event: CS Impressment Act (~50% below market)
timeline.effect: hoarding/evasion; city food supply falls

timeline.event: Richmond Bread Riot
timeline.effect: urban food access collapses amid rural plenty

timeline.event: CS Tax-in-Kind (tithe, 10%)
timeline.effect: adds collection (~$62M) but alienates farmers

timeline.event: Vicksburg + Gettysburg; Erlanger bond prices slide
timeline.effect: foreign credit window narrows

timeline.event: NYC Draft Riots
timeline.effect: political cost of the draft lever made visible

timeline.event: CS abolishes substitution
timeline.effect: closes class loophole; deepens elite resentment

timeline.event: CS Currency Reform Act (repudiates ~1/3 of money stock)
timeline.effect: brief price relief, permanent confidence damage

timeline.event: CS conscription expands to 17-50
timeline.effect: scrapes bottom of the manpower barrel

timeline.event: Union repeals $300 commutation (except COs)
timeline.effect: draft bites harder; bounty inflation

timeline.event: Greenback gold-value low (~39c) then recovers after Atlanta
timeline.effect: confidence trough; recovery with battlefield success

timeline.event: Sherman's Atlanta campaign on rebuilt rail; CS desertion accelerates; CS loses iron/rail districts
timeline.effect: CS replacement ratio falls below 1; production collapses regionally

timeline.event: Fort Fisher falls, closing Wilmington (last runner port)
timeline.effect: severs CS ocean lifeline; Erlanger bonds collapse

timeline.event: CS Black-soldier law (GO No. 14)
timeline.effect: too late: yields dozens

timeline.event: CS currency ~$1,200/gold; surrender; total monetary collapse (~9,000% cumulative)
timeline.effect: endgame: low-finance start matures into total failure

timeline.event: Union army at peak (~1M on rolls) musters out
timeline.effect: decisive end-state: Union still expanding while CS dissolves

teachingCardIndex.title: Why the greenback held value when the Confederate dollar collapsed
Did the Union 'win with money'?
The National Banking Acts: expedient or revolution?
Did finance lose the war, or did the war wreck finance?
King Cotton finance: brilliant collateral or fatal dependence?
The tax-that-wasn't: state capacity and the tithe
Was Confederate defeat foreordained by the industrial gap?
Gorgas and the miracle of Confederate ordnance
How the South actually armed itself
Material inevitability vs organizational failure (rail)
Did railroads decide the war, or merely shape it?
The gauge problem: how decisive was the 'break of gauge'?
Starvation or distribution failure? ('hunger amid plenty')
Did King Cotton ever have a chance, and was food the better bet?
Impressment and the tithe: necessary policy or self-inflicted famine?
How much did the reaper matter?
Did King Cotton diplomacy ever have a chance?
How decisive was the Union blockade?
Did blockade running help or hurt the Confederate war effort?
The Erlanger loan: lifeline or fleecing?
Did the Union draft actually supply soldiers?
'A rich man's war and a poor man's fight' — how unfair were the drafts?
Could the CS have solved its manpower crisis by arming the enslaved?
How decisive was Union demographic superiority?
How important were immigrants and USCT to Union manpower?
Was the South rich or poor in 1860?
Did the Union's material advantage make victory inevitable?
Confederate finance: doomed at the start, or mismanaged?

designerGaps: Exact 1864 CS cotton-bale figure (~300,000 cited) unconfirmed; war-total ~6.8M bales / ~3.3M destroyed better attested. Verify before using a single-year number.
Precise per-capita Southern corn (1860 census bushels) + year-by-year wartime output not pinned to a table.
Exact reduced CS ration ounce-weights post-spring-1862 are qualitative; consult OR Series I/IV commissary circulars for game ration tables.
Richmond Bread Riot crowd size varies (several hundred to 1,000+); use a range.

ALL game index/parameter scalings (production indices, distribution fractions, decay rates, variance %, replacement ratios, per-turn money/inflation rates) are inferred designer calibration starting points, NOT measured values. Playtest required.
'~25% British bread-grain from US' denominator (all food vs bread-grain vs wheat) varies by source.
turnCount unset: per-turn rates documented for a 16-turn war. Rescale inflationPerUnitPrinted, deteriorationPerTurnPct, and per-turn production/recruitment if the build uses a different turn count.
Slave-capital liquidity coefficient (~0.05) is a designer placeholder for the 'high wealth / low convertibility' mechanic; tune against the emancipation event swing.
